Name: Jahmyr Gibbs
Outlook: Perhaps our small brains cannot comprehend this man’s greatness in fantasy football. Gibbs is a superstar, totaling the most TDs (49) in NFL history by any RB through their first three years in the NFL. After Dan Campbell took over as play-caller (Week 10), Gibbs had the seventh most receptions of any player in the NFL as David Montgomery was phased out of the offense. Gibbs should see the biggest workload of his career and is a worthy selection at 1.01 this year. Consider your opponent’s pants pee-stained every time he touches the ball.
Dynasty: Expect a huge bag of money thrown his way over the summer. He is one of the most explosive players in the league and a surefire early first-round pick in startups. If you want to take him ahead of Bijan or one of the elite WRs, we do not blame you.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Bijan Robinson
Outlook: It was a video game-like 2025 season for Bijan, totaling 2,298 yards from scrimmage, the most since CMC in 2019. He accounted for 41% of Atlanta’s total team yardage thanks to ranking first among all RBs in forced missed tackles and percentage of the team’s first-read targets. If you are splitting hairs at the top of your drafts, the only knock on Bijan might be the functionality of the Atlanta offense and how the QB position shakes out. He is all that is great in fantasy football and a true building block if you have the pleasure of drafting him.
Dynasty: He is arguably the best offensive player in football, and he enters this offseason likely up for a major contract extension. Bijan should once again be featured in 2026 under new offensive play-callers Kevin Stefanski and Tommy Rees.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Christian McCaffrey
Outlook: This is the stuff fantasy dreams are made of. If you called your shot with CMC in 2025, oh baby, were you living large while the rest of your league mates cowered in fear due to “he’s gonna get injured” fears. The RB1 was a consistency monster as essentially two players in one for fantasy. Using only his receiving stats, he would have been the WR11 in half-point leagues or the WR8 in full PPR. He tied Amon-Ra St. Brown for the league lead in red zone receptions while totaling a whopping 413 touches, the most by a fantasy RB since DeMarco Murray in 2014. Whew! That type of workload suggests some regression is coming for the 30-year-old, but we still have him ranked as a top-5 RB. He is him, and his skill set for fantasy is like none other since the LaDanian Tomlinson days.
Dynasty: CMC is an all-timer for fantasy football. If you have ever had the opportunity to experience and inhabit a CMC #1 overall season, you likely won your league. However, we are not here to pat your back, but instead offer a word of caution. Since 2020, 11 RBs saw 330+ touches in a regular season; ALL of them saw a decrease of 45+ touches the following year, and only two played a full season. He can still be awesome for 2026, but we would advise shopping him in your league to get an absolute haul. This is “get paid” advice. 30-year-old RBs coming off this big of a workload have a tough time repeating.

Name: Jonathan Taylor
Outlook: Oh man, those first 10 weeks of JTT were legendary! His five 30+ fantasy point games were tied with 2000 Marshall Faulk and 2006 LaDanian Tomlinson for the most ever through the first ten weeks of a season. As a receiver, he was more involved than ever, setting career highs in targets, receptions, and receiving yards. Unfortunately, the Colts’ offense tanked down the stretch with Philip Rivers & Riley Leonard under center. Taylor’s production essentially was cut in half, nosediving from 23.3 fantasy points per game with Daniel Jones to 11.8 over those final five games. JTT is still a locked-in RB1 for us, although the first month of the season is a brutal stretch for the Colts.
Dynasty: In dynasty, this is the time if you aren’t a contender to trade the 27-year-old back for a haul. Taylor is in his final year under contract, but the Indianapolis front office could clean house this offseason if Shane Steichen fails to make the playoffs.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: James Cook III
Outlook: After agreeing to a four-year contract extension in mid-August, James Cook punched every "TD-regression" metric in the face in 2025, leading the league in rushing yards and finishing as a top-6 fantasy back. He paced the league with 11 games of 80+ yards rushing and ranked 3rd in yards after contact. The only stain on Cook’s resume is his declining involvement in the passing game, as he averaged just 1.9 receptions per game last year. Fantasy managers can have confidence in the continuity of this Buffalo offense, the play-calling, and an offensive line that continues to maul opposing fronts. Cook remains a strong RB1 for us in 2026.
Dynasty: Over the last two seasons, his 28 total rushing TDs rank fourth among all RBs. He is tailor-made for Joe Brady's zone scheme and is under contract until 2030. At just 26 years old and tied to Josh Allen, he remains a steady dynasty asset as a low-end RB1.

Name: De'Von Achane
Outlook: Coming off one of the most consistent fantasy seasons for an RB of all-time, hopefully, any doubts you had about Achane should be buried deep in the ground. He became one of four RBs ever to score 11+ fantasy points in every single game and ranked #1 in yards per carry (5.7) and explosive rush rate (10%) among all qualifying RBs. When you mix in his receiving work, he is a cheat code at the position. Despite Miami’s low win expectation for 2026, we are still in on his price tag at the 1/2 turn in draft as a locked-in RB1.
Dynasty: Achane signed a four-year, $68M extension, making him one of the highest-paid RBs in the league. While the Dolphins might be going through a rebuild, his dynasty value remains stable as a top-5 RB in startups. The pass-catching cheat code that he is will help him age well in this format.

Name: Ashton Jeanty
Outlook: Let’s be clear: Jeanty checks nearly every box we want in fantasy. Jeanty was actually Jason’s ‘Bust’ pick last year on the show, relative to ADP. It is not because we all didn’t love the talent and draft capital, but it is hard to be a fantasy difference maker at the RB position when your surroundings are as bad as they were a season ago. The O-line was a disaster, Pete Carroll ran a completely unserious operation, and Geno Smith regressed massively. It led to Las Vegas ranking dead last in TDs per game. Again, it is hard to be a great fantasy RB when that’s the case. Fast forward one year, and suddenly, we are more optimistic about where the Raiders are headed. Sure, Fernando Mendoza is a bit of a question mark, but the team put significant investment in the O-line, and we are big fans of Klint Kubiak’s scheme. Look for Jeanty to make a big-time jump in his efficiency metrics in Year 2, and if the Raiders offense can just be around league average, he could push for a top-8 RB season. Beyond the 29 rushing TDs last year as a Heisman runner-up, he is also one of three first-round RBs to see 20+ of their team’s receiving yardage in a single season (2023), joining some guys named Christian McCaffrey and Jahmyr Gibbs. The Raiders certainly were smitten, taking him higher than any RB in the NFL draft since Saquon Barkley in 2018. If you possess this type of draft capital (sixth overall) and elite college production at this young of an age (21 years old), you crush for fantasy. Expect a mammoth workload on a much-improved Raiders team. We have him as a fringe first-round pick in redraft, but certainly understand if you want to call your shot ahead of other established backs.
Dynasty: The only RBs above Jeanty in our consensus start-up rankings are Jahmyr Gibbs and Bijan Robinson. That tells you all you need to know about Jeanty’s future in dynasty leagues. As a prospect, he was one of those rare talents with top-10 draft capital, so you can feel pretty confident you will get multiple high-end fantasy seasons from Ashton Jeanty.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Derrick Henry
Outlook: You can tell your grandkids you lived through the glory years known as "Derrick Henry SZN". He is an absolute fantasy legend finishing 2025 with his eighth straight season of double-digit rushing TDs. He saw the most carries inside the 5-yard line of his career and ranked third among all RBs in yards per carry. “Father Time” is usually undefeated when it comes to 30+ year old RBs, but Henry has kicked him outside the wrestling ring the last couple of years. Our projections still have him ranked as a low-end RB1 with a slight deduction in full PPR leagues.
Dynasty: The future Hall of Famer is under contract for two more years as he carries a team-friendly $5.5 million cap hit in 2026 that balloons to $16.2 in 2027.  It is worth noting that since 2010, only one 32+ year old RB has had a double-digit TD season: DeAngelo Williams (PIT) in 2015 had 11 rushing TDs. If you have Henry in dynasty, you could entertain offers or just ride this man into the sunset!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kenneth Walker III
Outlook: Ken Bone! Walker was a key piece of the Seattle offense down the stretch and parlayed his Super Bowl MVP award by receiving a big old bag of money from the Chiefs. He signed a three-year deal worth just over $14M per year. He is still only 25 years old and remains one of the best home run hitters in the game, something that was severely lacking in KC's run game over the last two years. For context, over the last two years, Chiefs RBs have run for 10+ yards less than 9% of the time, 31st amongst 32 teams. Enter Walker, who was third in the league last season in rate of 10+ yard runs and #1 in forced missed tackle rate. Expect 18-20 touches per game as the team re-establishes the run game and the red zone approach under OC Eric Bienemy en route to RB1 status for the Bone Zone.
Dynasty: If you have Ken Walker on your dyno squad, you have our permission to join the Hitman in removing your pants at this time. This was an incredible run out for a player who always tantalized with his per-touch ability. Only 25 years old, he is a top-10 dynasty RB in startups for us.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Omarion Hampton
Outlook: It was a frustrating rookie season for Omarion Hampton, who missed Weeks 6-13 with a small fracture in his ankle. However, when he was on the field, you saw flashes of what he can do in fantasy. He only appeared in nine games, but in those contests, Hampton handled a 30% share of the team’s total rush attempts and targets. For context, anything at or above 30% in that metric is elite and quite rare. Now entering Year 2 in the league, there is room for that number to grow, and if it does in this Mike McDaniel scheme with a healthier offensive line, Hampton is a strong candidate to finish inside the top 10 at the position.
Dynasty: We are not ready to put Hampton up there with guys like Bijan and Gibbs, but Hampton is a surefire RB1 in dynasty startups. He is only 23 years old and has that first-round draft capital that suggests he will have multiple seasons as LA’s bell cow. If you have him, ride this thing out for the next two to three years, knowing you have one of dynasty’s most valuable RBs.

Name: Chase Brown
Outlook: It looked like Brown might end up one of 2025’s biggest fantasy busts after averaging just 8.1 fantasy points per game on 51 total yards through the first five weeks of the season. He ended up becoming a league winner en route to seeing the fourth-most targets among all RBs while averaging 100 total yards per game from Week 6 on. The only yellow flag in his profile is his lack of major success inside the 5-yard line. Cincinnati failed to bring in any other significant RB competition, making Brown yet again a candidate to finish as an RB1 for the third year in a row as part of this Joe Burrow-led offense.
Dynasty: Brown survived yet another off-season where the Bengals failed to add a challenger in the RB room. As a PPR monster attached to a high-powered, Brown is in a sweet spot for fantasy as we consider him a fringe top-10 dynasty RB. Expect a potential off-season extension to surface this summer.

Name: Saquon Barkley
Outlook: Coming off a legendary 2024 campaign and Super Bowl run, there were warning signs about regression coming across the board for Saquon and the Eagles' offense. He bottomed out in a number of areas, including plummeting from 17 runs of 20+ yards to only four in 2025. He also shockingly converted only one of his 13 carries inside the 5-yard line into a TD, but still volumed his way to finishing as the RB13. With an offensive coordinator change, we expect Saquon to bounce back in 2026 at a discounted price in the second round of your drafts. Your league mates might bring up the fact he was a “bust,” but we are willing to forgive and forget.
Dynasty: Entering his age-29 season, this is the time to make a decision on Saquon based on your team’s competitive window. If you are a contender, you can expect one more year of elite production, but rebuilds should consider shopping him for at least a first-round pick and another young piece. He is under contract for two more seasons, but we are not valuing him as an elite asset beyond 2026.

Name: Josh Jacobs
Outlook: It looked like Jacobs was going to repeat as a certifiable RB1 for fantasy, averaging 22 opportunities and 1.2 rushing TDs per game through the first ten weeks of the season. He hit a major wall down the stretch, dealing with a knee injury and averaging just 10.5 fantasy points from Week 13 on. We know what he is: a TD monster with a league-leading 24 rushing TDs inside the 10-yard line over the last two seasons. The workload should be there, but we are slightly lower than where he is being drafted, viewing the aging RB as more of a fringe top-12 guy.
Dynasty: While his contract says he has two more years left in Lambeau, there is language that would allow the Packers to get out after this year if he falters. At age 28, he fits the bill as someone moving along the age curve, and it is better to get out ahead of his dynasty lifecycle.

Name: Kyren Williams
Outlook: Ever since Sean McVay gave Kyren the reins, he has been amazing for fantasy, finishing as a top-10 back in three straight seasons. He is rarely hitting big plays or flashing elite speed in the open field, but he is as dependable as they come, and McVay trusts this dude. Blake Corum is probably going to steal a few TDs and work in for a handful of touches per game, but when the game is on the line, McVay is calling Kyren’s number. In 2026, he is projected as a volume-based high-end RB2 who could bank another RB1 finish if the TDs go his way.
Dynasty: Williams signed a three-year extension with the Rams, but fantasy managers should really treat this like a two-year window. He is already entering his age-26 season.

Name: Jeremiyah Love
Outlook: The Notre Dame superstar became the highest draft RB since Saquon Barkley after the Cardinals selected him third overall. His combination of size, speed, early breakout age (19.3), and production is a recipe for success at the next level. However, with Tyler Allgeier and James Conner on the roster as well as a bevy of legit pass-catchers, we might need to temper expectations for fantasy. His weekly workload will be in question despite the fact that RBs drafted inside the top 10 have averaged 19+ touches per game in Year 1 over the last decade. We have Love ranked as an RB2 for 2026.
Dynasty: While the landing spot might not seem ideal in the present, we are not worried at all about Love’s long-term trajectory. Consider how Bijan had a similar beginning with Allgeier stealing goal-line looks alongside the all-world RB. We have him firmly ranked inside our top-5 RBs in startups and a true building block in dynasty.

Name: Cam Skattebo
Outlook: How good was the Skatt man for the 10 weeks of the season? He was the RB10, averaging 14.5 fantasy points per game, including a month’s stretch where the Giants fed him the ball 22+ times a game. His gruesome ankle fracture was tough to watch, as his energy and enthusiasm made him one of our favorite players to watch in 2025. He came out saying he would be ready for Week 1, but we would expect his best football to be played in the back half of the season, as it may take time to regain his explosiveness and dynamic ability. Consider him an RB2 for your fantasy squads on a run-heavy Giants team with an improved offensive line and a new scheme.
Dynasty: Before going down to injury, he ripped off six straight top-24 weekly finishes among RBs, so he clearly has the skillset to succeed in the game we play. That said, whenever there is a regime change, the dynasty value for Day 3 RBs is always a bit murky. For now, he is the clear best fantasy option in the backfield and a mid-RB2 in our startup rankings.

Name: Breece Hall
Outlook: After signing a three-year extension following a tumultuous offseason, it is clear Breece remains the Jets’ main guy in the backfield. The opportunities to score were slim in 2025 as the Jets were tied or trailing on 93% of their offensive snaps, the worst rate in the league. Zoom out a bit further, and you will see that the team ranked dead last in rush attempts inside the 5-yard line for the third year in a row. His volume and breakaway run rate can make up for the team’s shortcomings. With a 45+ reception floor, Hall can be a valuable RB2 and potentially finish as an RB1 if the TDs finally fall in his favor.
Dynasty: It feels like Breece has already lived nine lives in the NFL (playing for the Jets will do that), but he is still not even 25 years old. One of the most productive and efficient backs over the last three seasons, the Jets rewarded Breece with a brand new three-year deal worth just over $15 million per year. Some dynasty managers out there may have been hoping for Breece to finally be unleashed on a new team and hopefully a better offense, but this contract says he will get plenty of volume in New York.

Name: Bucky Irving
Outlook: While the majority of 2025 fantasy RBs stayed healthy, Irving dealt with foot and shoulder injuries, limiting him to just 10 games. He was extremely ineffective, ranking 51st in rush success rate and 47th in explosive rush rate among 55 qualifying RBs. The Buccaneers also failed to give him a single carry inside the 5-yard line all season! We still believe in the talent, but Irving is shaping up to be one of the bigger wildcard picks in the early rounds. Kenneth Gainwell will certainly steal passing downs work, and Sean Tucker was re-signed to be the Bucs’ red-zone hammer. If you still are bullish on Bucky, shoot your shot as your team’s RB2 in hopes the entire offense resets in 2026.
Dynasty: From Day 3 pick to dynasty darling, Bucky’s rough 2025 campaign can be chalked up to injuries, a new play-caller, and a spell of unfortunate TD luck. We have him firmly ranked as a high-end RB2 in dynasty startups, given his age (24 years old) and the game-breaking talent he displayed as a rookie.

Name: Javonte Williams
Outlook: One of the best fantasy stories of 2025 had to be the resurgence of Javonte Williams as Dallas' RB1. Among qualified RBs, Javonte was fourth in yards after contact per attempt while seeing 19 opportunities per game. For the first four years of his frustrating career, he had eight total TDs inside the 5-yard line; in 2025, he had 10! With no direct competition for touches, Javonte is a solid RB2 as part of a Dallas offense we love buying into for fantasy.
Dynasty: We did it! The Javonte hive that held strong after years of disappointment had its breakthrough in dynasty.  After a bounce-back season, Dallas rewarded him with a three-year extension that includes $16 million in guaranteed money. His receiving efficiency was puzzling as he totaled a putrid 137 receiving yards on 51 targets, the fewest of any player with 50+ targets over the last 40 years! Expect at least two years of legitimate production.

Name: Quinshon Judkins
Outlook: Judkins was everything the Browns envisioned when they selected him 34th overall in last year’s draft. Despite the team’s lack of success, he was a workhorse, averaging 18.3 touches per game and 34% of the team's total rush attempts and targets before a brutal dislocated ankle and fractured fibula ended his season in Week 16. He has a chance to be cleared for Week 1, though he will not be 100% when the season begins. Expect his production to pick up in the second half of the season, making him an awkward selection in the middle rounds of your draft.
Dynasty: He is expected to make a full recovery for the 2026 season and should be viewed as a top-12 dynasty RB even on a bad football team. He has the size and three-down skillset to be an elite performer for the next handful of years.

Name: D'Andre Swift
Outlook: Over the last six years, only two RBs finished inside the top 24 at the position every year: Derrick Henry and D'Andre Swift. Yep, it isn’t always the sexiest outcomes, but Swift is the clear lead back for a Chicago offense we want pieces of in fantasy. He set career highs in rushing yards, rushing TDs, and rush success rate behind a much- improved Bears offensive line. Over the back half of the season, he did cede work to Kyle Monangai, but we still view Swift as the lead guy in a 60-40 backfield. Does he make it seven years in a row as an RB2? Our projections certainly think so.

Name: TreVeyon Henderson
Outlook: Stop if you have heard this before: we love us some Hendo. It was a painfully slow start to his career, but he broke out in the second half as the RB8 from Week 9 on, averaging 16.7 fantasy points per game. When Rhamondre Stevenson was out for a month, he showcased bell cow capabilities, averaging 20+ opportunities per game and an 80% snap rate. We know he is explosive, but will the coaching staff trust him as the team’s main passing-downs back in 2026? He carries massive upside at his ADP, but we would recommend building out RB depth on your roster around him as you navigate the beginning of the season with Hendo.

We have him ranked as a top-15 RB in dynasty formats, but our projections might have him end up finishing lower for 2026.

Name: Travis Etienne Jr.
Outlook: After Jacksonville’s Week 8 bye, Etienne turned into a fantasy force, averaging 17.5 fantasy points per game as the RB7. He was a wild outlier as a pass-catcher, becoming the third RB over the last 25 years to score 6+ rec TDs on fewer than 40 receptions. The main headline for the man formerly known as Travis Etienne Jr. was the four-year free agent deal he signed to come home to New Orleans and be crowned Travis “Aychane” Jr. With Alvin Kamara phasing out of the team’s plans, Etienne will be given every opportunity to be a workhorse for this Kellen Moore-designed offense. We are not banking on anything of significant value after these first two years of the contract.

Name: Bhayshul Tuten

He was also much better inside the 10-yard line than Etienne, converting his eight rush attempts into five scores compared to 26 and only four. Name: David Montgomery
Outlook: The Texans must have a type: past-their-prime early down runners who can be effective scoring TDs in bunches. Montgomery's value received a major boost when he was traded from Detroit to Houston as the team was desperately searching for an answer inside the red zone. The team converted a league-worst 17% of their attempts inside the 5-yard line into TDs, with the league-average being 39%. Monty should see 18+ touches a game and return low-end RB2 value for a team that wants to win by running the ball and leaning on their defense.

Name: Chuba Hubbard
Outlook: The 27-year-old Chuba finds himself as the presumed lead back in Carolina a year after taking a backseat to Rico Dowdle. The efficiency numbers weren’t pretty, averaging a paltry 3.8 yards per carry with only 6% of his runs gaining 10+ yards, the fourth lowest rate among qualifying RBs. Name: Jadarian Price
Outlook: After much speculation before the NFL Draft, the Seahawks took the Notre Dame RB with the final pick of Round 1. Price’s selection looks even stronger, knowing we did not see another RB drafted until the end of Round 3. His initial burst plus his contact balance make for a lethal combo, although he was not utilized much in the passing game in college, with just 15 total receptions over three years. With Zach Charbonnet on the mend from an ACL injury, Price found arguably the best landing spot for a rookie RB in 2026. The Seahawks are favored in each of their first 10 matchups. We have him ranked as a high-end FLEX option with the expectation that his draft stock could rise throughout the summer.
Dynasty: As Winston Churchill once said, some things are "a riddle, wrapped in a mystery, inside an enigma.” Price was a confusing prospect to evaluate as the backup to Jeremiyah Love with minimal college production, and yet became only the second RB over the last decade drafted in Rounds 1-3 that had fewer than 700 rushing yards in his final college season. Opportunity is everything, and on a Super Bowl-winning team, he should have the chance to be this team’s lead back of the future.

Name: Jaylen Warren
Outlook: Warren had a high floor throughout the season, averaging 16 opportunities per game and 12.3 fantasy points per game while receiving a “B” in our consistency metrics. While he ceded passing downs work to Kenneth Gainwell down the stretch, it was the best fantasy season of Warren’s four-year career, thanks to an increase in workload. He was rather pedestrian inside the 5-yard line, converting only four of his 14 carries. His outlook for 2026 is souring with the signing of Rico Dowdle and the arrival of a new coaching regime. Name: Rhamondre Stevenson

Name: Rico Dowdle
Outlook: After Chuba Hubbard was hurt in Week 5 last year, Rico essentially stole the RB1 role in Carolina; he was highly productive with his opportunities, mixing in some wild 30+ fantasy point spike weeks. Mr. "Two Pumps" Dowdle rushed for 1,076 yards this past season and ran for a career high six TDs as the leader of the committee in Carolina. Dowdle has been the lead guy for two different teams now, and while he may not be an elite talent, the guy just gets the job done. He lands in Pittsburgh reunited with former coach Mike McCarthy alongside Jaylen Warren. Consider Rico a “FLEX with benefits” to start the year.
Dynasty: During the last two years in Dallas and Carolina, he showed he can be reliable with 200+ touches in each season. His two-year deal includes just $5 million guaranteed, and his cap hit in 2027 jumps to $8.5 million. Consider this a one-year experiment, hoping he can regain the magic he had with McCarthy in Dallas.

Name: Tony Pollard
Outlook: Pollard might have been the most boring fantasy selection at RB in 2025. The first 13 weeks were utter garbage as he averaged 15+ touches per game while turning that volume into a measly 7.9 fantasy points per game. Woof. He did save the best for last, turning it on in the final month of the fantasy season with four top-20 finishes in a row while averaging 113 rushing yards per game. The 29-year-old is hitting the crashing point in the lifecycle of an NFL RB and enters the final year of his Titans contract as the lead back on an offense that hopefully takes a step forward under new OC Brian Daboll. If dirty, dirty RB volume is your thing, Pollard can be a FLEX for your team for one last ride in 2026.
Dynasty: We are a long way from the high-impact, efficiency monster he was behind Zeke in Dallas. He enters the final season of his free agent deal, sputtering to the finish line. The Titans are favored in just one of their first six games, so pray Pollard falls into the end zone.

Name: Kyle Monangai
Outlook: The seventh-round Monangai was a revelation, tag-teaming with Swift as the Bears posted the sixth-most total rushing yards in the league. Monangai was a machine, gaining 5+ yards on 42.6% of his carries, the second-highest rate in the NFL behind only Kyren Williams. He certainly has juice with the upside to see a big workload if Swift went down, as evidenced by a Week 9 game where Ben Johnson turned full sicko, feeding Monangai the ball 31(!) times. Consider Monangai a “FLEX with benefits” later in your draft.
Dynasty: It is tough to gauge Monangai’s long-term value as his draft capital suggests this team could draft a high-end back in 2027 once Swift is a free agent. For now, he sits inside our top-36 RBs in startups, knowing there is volatility moving forward.

The subject of Footballers fame burst onto the scene last summer as a seventh-round NFL draft pick and ultimately lost his starting job to Chris Rodriguez Jr. He converted just three of his 12 carries inside the 5-yard line to TDs last year and now finds more competition in the backfield with Rachaad White’s signing and rookie Kaytron Allen. While things look good on paper for Bill entering 2026, this is a buyer-beware situation. Washington is favored in just two of its first 10 games.

Name: RJ Harvey

However, after Dobbins went down with yet another season-ending injury, Harvey averaged 18.4 opportunities, 17 fantasy points per game, and finished the season with 12 total TDs. Those scores might be propping him up in fantasy managers’ minds despite the fact that his efficiency marks were less than stellar as a runner. Sean Payton seems committed to a RB committee, bringing back J.K. Dobbins while adding fourth- round pick Jonah Coleman. Harvey is in FLEX territory, but he might be maddening as a start/sit decision every week.

He did see 58 targets (tied for the fifth most among Day 2 RBs over the last decade), giving you hope that his value will hold in full PPR leagues. Nevertheless, he likely saw his value peak already in dynasty.

Name: Jordan Mason
Outlook: Mason continues to show he belongs in the NFL, averaging 5.1 yards per carry since coming into the league in 2022. For context, only De’Von Achane (5.6) and Jahmyr Gibbs (5.3) are higher in that span. After averaging 16 opportunities per game through the first six games, he eventually fell behind Aaron Jones Sr. in the RB rotation down the stretch in a lost season for the Vikings. Mason is older than you might realize (27 years old), but is only an injury away from being an every-down workhorse.
Dynasty: Mason is a fun dynasty story to share as savvy managers read the tea leaves of the last few years and hopefully recouped value as a startable RB2/FLEX when needed. Signed in free agency last year, Mason has one more year on his deal before the void years kick in. He carries a number of rushing yardage incentives (800/900/1,000 yards) in his contract that could come into play down the stretch.

Name: J.K. Dobbins

He signed a two-year deal worth up to $20 million to run it back with the Broncos. While we still expect Harvey to see high-value looks in the pass game, Dobbins should have FLEX-worthy weeks in the right matchup while running behind one of the best offensive lines in football.
Dynasty: Dobbins keeps picking up “extra lives,” proving these last two years that he can still contribute as a fantasy RB2 in a timeshare. He is a hold in dynasty, knowing the sun will be setting soon for a player plagued with injuries.

Of course, most of us were not expecting much from Gainwell given his track record and the minimal investment, but goodness gracious, he was a force in PPR formats thanks to his team-leading 73 receptions. Name: Blake Corum
Outlook: The Rams finally did it. They lessened the workload on Kyren Williams in 2025 after hinting at it for a couple of seasons. In 2024, when Corum was a rookie, he wasn’t a factor at all. However, in his sophomore campaign, he siphoned off 159 total opportunities for a 35% share of the carries. Kyren is obviously the lead back in this offense, but it is entirely possible Corum pushes that number closer to the 40 - 45% mark. If he does, he could have standalone value as a weekly FLEX play. Of course, should Kyren go down, Corum would project as a no-brainer weekly RB1, so he is also one of fantasy’s most valuable insurance policies while playing for one of the best offenses in the league.

Name: Aaron Jones Sr.
Outlook: After averaging 4.9 yards per carry for the first eight seasons of his career, Aaron Jones finally hit a wall in Year 9, setting career lows in yards per carry (4.2), rushing TDs (2), and yards after contact per attempt (2.67). He remains under contract for one more year in Minnesota, sharing the backfield with Jordan Mason. The Vikings begin the season with a rough three-week stretch (GB, @ CHI, @ TB) before things open up. We have him ranked as a low-end FLEX option with unknowns at the QB and betting that the wheels finally fall off of his insanely efficient career on a per-touch basis.
Dynasty: Jones is hitting that RB age cliff (31 years old) and only has one more year on his deal. You likely will get nothing of value in a trade, so we recommend holding in hopes he bounces back in the TD department and the Minnesota offense returns to its norm with Kyler Murray under center.

Name: Tyrone Tracy Jr.

He certainly is capable of being a fantasy-friendly asset thanks to his pass-catching abilities, but we project him to firmly be the RB2 behind Skattebo. In the later rounds of your drafts, we do not mind adding Tracy to your bench if you think they lean on him at the beginning of the year as Skatt ramps up.
Dynasty: A tale as old as time: young RB drafted on Day 3 has a hot streak, and then dynasty managers elevate him higher than they should. Tracy was a nice story for the woeful 2024 Giants, but it is clear he was never the long-term answer for this roster or in dynasty. RBs with this type of investment at the NFL level are expendable and easily replaced.

Name: James Conner
Outlook: It has been a wonderful run for Conner, but it seems he finally will fade into the fantasy background. After four straight years as a top-20 RB and the engine of the Arizona offense, the 31-year-old missed the majority of 2025 with a right foot injury, and it seems he will be buried on the depth chart behind Tyler Allgeier and rookie Jeremiyah Love.
Dynasty: Conner is under contract for one more year. Rather than moving on from the 31-year-old and saving over $7 million against the cap, the team decided to restructure his deal to keep him on the roster for 2026.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Braelon Allen
Outlook: Honest question: Do you have any memory of Allen contributing in the 2025 season? If you do, you are either a “Rain Man” or a diabolical liar, as he touched the ball 20 total times before bowing out for the rest of the season to an MCL sprain. He is a man-child and an impressive physical specimen, but likely nothing more than a dart throw in your drafts.
Dynasty: At just 22 years old, Allen remains one of the youngest RBs in the league despite having “two years” of NFL experience. You can start connecting the dots with a tin foil hat on and see a path for Allen to be a fantasy monster if Breece Hall went down.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Sean Tucker
Outlook: Tucker had a few shining moments in 2025, including a monster RB1 overall performance in Buffalo in Week 11. He can flash at times and also led the team in carries (14) and rushing TDs (6) inside the 10-yard line. However, the Buccaneers backfield needs to move from a three-headed monster (Bucky, Kenny G & the Tuckmeister) to two in order for him to be started with any confidence. Expect Tucker to be floating on waiver wires after your drafts.
Dynasty: Tucker signed a one-year deal worth $3.52 million to return as the team’s preferred goal-line back. There is a place for that on a dynasty bench, as he is better suited for standard or half-point formats.

Name: DJ Giddens
Outlook: The former fifth-round pick is the presumed backup to Jonathan Taylor in Indy. However, we confess that his rookie season does not give us a ton of information to work from, as he touched the ball 26 total times and was active in three total games from Week 8 on. He is an explosive athlete with the opportunity to lock up the RB2 spot. For now, know the name, but the JTT manager should not feel obligated to draft him even as insurance.
Dynasty: The size (6’0”, 212 lbs.) and speed combo (4.43 40-time) made Giddens an intriguing dynasty name a year ago. If Taylor were to go down, he could vault into being an every-down guy, but that remains mere speculation. In the words of Cosmo Kramer, “Giddy Up,” DJ!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Kaelon Black
Outlook: Despite a lackluster production profile, Black was a hot commodity in pre-draft visits, meeting with 10 teams before the 49ers surprisingly selected him 90th overall. Kyle Shanahan offenses always surprise us with the names that leap from obscurity, but this one was a head scratcher. CMC is coming off a season where he touched the ball 413 times, so perhaps we see Black worked into the mix. Pay attention to camp reports to see where he lands on the depth chart, competing with Jordan James and Isaac Guerendo for backup duties.
Dynasty: The Indiana RB was the lesser-known of their National Championship-winning backfield duo, and yet Roman Hemby went undrafted. We have a history of failed 49ers RB draft picks, but Black should be evaluated based on his profile, not their lack of success. He belongs in the mid-second round of drafts, but RB-thirsty managers might push him higher.

Name: Ray Davis
Outlook: After a steady rookie season backing up James Cook, Davis saw his usage curiously cut in half last year, averaging just 15% of the snaps and just four touches per game despite dressing for all 17 regular-season games. He is nothing more than an insurance policy for Cook managers as he fights for playing time alongside third-down back Ty Johnson.
Dynasty: Entering Year 3, Davis has a tough path to carving out any fantasy relevance. His early down skillset is directly blocked by Cook and Buffalo’s true goal-line back, Josh Allen. If Cook were to miss time, we would assume Davis would be the heir apparent to being the lead back in this Buffalo rushing attack.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Brashard Smith
Outlook: The seventh-round pick from 2025 was a trendy sleeper pick last year, as fantasy managers cited “well, Isaiah Pacheco was a seventh-round pick too!” Smith was sparingly used all season, touching the ball 79 total times despite the uninspiring play of veterans Kareem Hunt and Pacheco. With Kenneth Walker III now aboard, he is back to being a rotational back with little path forward in fantasy.
Dynasty: Welp, there goes his window in dynasty. The former WR turned RB tantalized with his production profile and speed (4.39 40-time), but is barely holding value as a bench player. Do not feel the need to roster him apart from the deepest of PPR leagues.

Name: Jaylen Wright
Outlook: After two seasons backing up De’Von Achane in Miami, it is clear Wright is not cut from the same cloth. He was drafted for his speed (4.38 40-time), but is averaging only 3.9 yards per carry and caught eight total passes. He is a backup RB on a team currently favored in just one of their 17 games. Translation: he is not on our redraft radar.
Dynasty: With Mike McDaniel out the door, any semblance of hope you had for the Dolphins carrying a 1-2 punch at RB is gonezo. The curse of players coming from the University of Tennessee's college offensive system continues to plague teams that drafted them. He is rosterable but will not be usable apart from an Achane injury.

Name: Emanuel Wilson
Outlook: Wilson was quietly better than Josh Jacobs on a number of efficiency metrics. He is coming off a career high 142 total opportunities in Green Bay and flashed in a major way with his lone start in Week 12 with Jacobs sidelined. His addition to the Seattle backfield is noteworthy given Kenneth Walker’s departure and Zach Charbonnet’s ACL tear. There is a massive opportunity up for grabs in this Seahawks backfield, although first-round pick Jadarian Price should eventually be the lead back. Wilson could gain more touches than we think early on, but he likely fades into the background once Charbs returns from injury.
Dynasty: The 27-year-old signed a low-risk one-year deal worth up to $2.1 million. That contract and the drafting of Jadarian Price suggest he is nothing more than a depth piece for this squad.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Isaiah Davis
Outlook: Davis is an explosive backup RB who averaged 5.5 yards per carry through two seasons, albeit on just 73 total attempts. He was also electric in the passing game, but averaged just 29% of the snaps behind Breece Hall. New OC Frank Reich and HC Aaron Glenn have toiled with the idea of a committee approach, but the contract extension and talent of Hall suggest otherwise. Expect a handful of touches per game but zero fantasy relevance to start the season.
Dynasty: The 24-year-old Davis enters Year 3 stuck behind Breece Hall, competing for bread crumbs alongside Braelon Allen. He sadly ended up on the wrong NFL team to make significant fantasy noise. For dynasty purposes, he is barely holding a bench spot, even in the deepest of leagues.
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Devin Neal
Outlook: The sixth-round pick in 2025 barely sniffed the field for the first three months of the season behind veteran Alvin Kamara. However, he was called upon for a three-week stretch (Weeks 12-14) when he averaged 17 opportunities per game before missing the final two weeks of the season due to an injury. Perhaps he can carve out a role as the RB2 behind Etienne as the insurance back, but he is best left on waivers to start the year.
Dynasty: Consider him the “Anthony Mackie” of Avengers actors: he’s fine, but when you give him a leading role, you are left asking why anyone greenlit this as a good idea. Neal was an all-around back at Kansas who didn’t exactly overpower people as a runner. With Etienne inked to a big free agent deal, he is relegated to being a middling backup.

Name: Jerome Ford
Outlook: “Uncle Jerome” had a brief moment filling in Cleveland a couple of years back, but at this point, he is nothing more than another camp body. Someone in the Washington backfield will get the short end of the stick after training camp and pre-season. Sorry, Jerome, but everyone is pointing at you.
Dynasty: The former fifth-rounder was once useful on the Browns, including a surprising RB17 season in 2023. His days of fantasy relevance and taking up an NFL roster spot are likely coming to an end.

Name: Jaydon Blue
Outlook: Blue was a hyped name last summer as a fifth-round rookie, some claimed could take hold of the Dallas backfield. Whoops! He took a backseat to Javonte Williams while being inactive in 12 games last season. We are projecting Blue to take a small step forward and potentially see 20-25% of the backfield touches in 2026. For fantasy, he is off the redraft radar, but perhaps his receiving work eats into Javonte’s.
Dynasty: Blue is a classic example of dynasty managers getting the cart before the horse. While the opportunity last off-season was there, the draft capital (149th overall pick) suggested he would likely not see the field much as a rookie. Consider him an insurance back, although Malik Davis could certainly be the preferred lead back if Javonte went down.

Name: Devin Singletary
Outlook: Entering the final year of his free agent deal with the Giants, “Singlecarry” was surpassed on the depth chart by both Cam Skattebo and Tyrone Tracy while seeing career lows in receptions and yards per carry. It’s not the way you want things to trend entering your age-29 season. Consider this man D-O-N-E.
Dynasty: “Dingleberry” was a Daboll guy, so it wouldn't be surprising to see him completely phased out of the offense with Skatt and Tracy dominating touches. He is a fringe bench name who likely will be on dynasty waiver wires heading into 2027.

Name: Kaytron Allen
Outlook: Nicknamed "the Fatman" by his mother, Allen came into Penn State as a highly regarded recruit and got to work in the gym. He checks a lot of the physical profile boxes we want at the next level, and finished his college career as Penn State's all-time leader in rush attempts and rushing yards. The question is whether his lack of flash and creativity as a runner will cap him at the next level. He likely begins third or fourth on the Washington depth chart.
Dynasty: While playing alongside Nicholas Singleton, he certainly was able to handle a heavy workload, and his final three-game run (72 carries for 567 yards and five rushing TDs) put an exclamation point on a stellar career. His patient, no-nonsense style will win over coaches, but as an older prospect, it is tough pounding the table for him despite being in a subpar RB draft class.

Name: Trevor Etienne
Outlook: Travis’ little brother was a fourth-rounder out of Georgia last year who touched the ball only 23 times as a rookie. He will compete with Jonathon Brooks for touches behind Chuba Hubbard. That is fantasy football code for: he is probably irrelevant.
Dynasty: Oh, you are looking for Trevor (“Aychane”) Etienne dynasty advice?! He was a middling prospect stuck in depth chart purgatory in Carolina. If his name were “Donald McDonaldson,” you would probably think twice, but the family name subconsciously gives him a boost that maybe he could work in fantasy football.

Name: George Holani
Outlook: GEORGE! The Seahawks special teamer was thrust into a bigger role during the NFL playoffs after Zach Charbonnet went down with his ACL tear. He caught three passes in the NFC title game but will once again be relegated to third or fourth string on the roster. Rookie Jadarian Price and Emmanuel Wilson should work ahead of George, with Charbonnet eventually returning from injury.
Dynasty: There is a small Holani hive that meets in the shadows, begging and demanding that their hero be given more opportunities on the field. They are a selective group believing the former undrafted free agent will rise from the depths and eventually take over an NFL backfield. “He’s good”, they say, referencing his Week 10 TD run against Arizona. “George is electric!” writes an anonymous group leader in their AOL group chat forum, mentioning his 24.2 yards per kick return average in 2025. “The government is suppressing his Boise State college film!" adds a user named “FFHitman”. Who are we to judge if this is the “Summer of George”? He is under contract for one more season for a whopping $1 million and will be a restricted free agent in 2027.

Name: Will Shipley
Outlook: Competing with Tank Bigsby for scraps at the table, Shipley had just 23 total touches on the season for 105 total yards. To put his brief two-year career in perspective, his 27% rush success rate ranks 419th among 425 qualifying RBs since 2010. Apologies if you were coming to Will Shipley’s player profile hoping for some optimism. It ain’t here, folks!
Dynasty: Shipley is nothing but an insurance back to dangle in front of a Saquon manager. We often overvalue these backups as the fear of “what could happen” outweighs the reality of a former Day 3 RB ever making an impact in dynasty. His capable hands make him a third-down back to stash for now.

Name: Kaleb Johnson
Outlook: Should we just act like 2025 never happened? The Steelers drafted Johnson in the third round, and then it was all downhill from there. His kickoff blunder in Week 1 against Seattle set the stage for a train-wreck of a season where he was inactive in seven games and failed to have a single rush attempt gain 10+ yards. If there is any hope for the future, it is the fact that Mike Tomlin is gone, and Mike McCarthy could give him a clean slate. He begins the off-season as the projected RB3 in Pittsburgh.
Dynasty: That sound you heard is the garbage truck passing your house as all your Kaleb Johnson dynasty shares are compacted into a worthless mess. He is still a hold because we all know “you can’t cut that guy”. Our studies show that Day 2 RBs who were sparingly used as a rookie (<70 total touches) often end up increasing in Year 2 with a double-digit per-game workload. Good luck!

Name: AJ Dillon
Outlook: The artist formerly known as “AJ Villain” is in Year 6 of the feature film production known as “Quadzilla”. While there was a ton of hype early on for this movie, the reviews are in: Dillon is better suited for the “hired muscle” role than as a featured final boss. He technically is on the Carolina roster, but could easily be cut come August with just $100,000 guaranteed in his contract.
Dynasty: Ah, so you’ve come here for AJ Dillon dynasty wisdom. How is this guy still in the NFL? This is AJ Dillon’s profile for fantasy football. Nothing could be more serious than this man’s contribution to fantasy rosters in 2026… except for the fact that there is a real chance he doesn’t make the final 53-man roster.

Name: Ollie Gordon II
Outlook: The 6’2” bruiser saw limited work as a rookie, averaging an utterly disgusting 2.8 yards per carry on 70 total carries. Sounds bad, right? What if we told you that was the WORST per carry average of any rookie RB over the last decade? Seriously, 99 different rookie RBs since 2016 saw 70+ carries, and Ollie was the worst. It was quite the dubious distinction. Are you curious who the best rookie was in that span? (Seriously, since you are here wanting Ollie Gordon II takes, we might as well keep this thing rolling.) It was some guy named De’Von Achane who averaged five MORE yards per carry (7.8) than Ollie did. His sixth-round draft capital, Achane’s insane talent, and the Dolphins' projected win total (4.5) suggest you will not be counting on Ollie in 2026.
Dynasty: Remember when Gordon was a sophomore sensation at Oklahoma State? His name value keeps him afloat, but as a backup RB on arguably the worst roster in the NFL, what are we going for here, folks? Are you sincerely hoping for an Achane injury? You sick, sick person. Leave. Now!
Markers: Mark Drafted                      Mark Keeper                      Mark Favorite                      Mark Watchlist                      Mark Avoid

Name: Adam Randall
Outlook: Randall came to Clemson as a highly regarded WR and ended his senior year as an intriguing RB prospect after collecting 13 total TDs and 36 receptions. He was taken on Day 3, most notably as someone hand-selected by the team’s owner, which is either the best thing or the worst thing, depending on your point of view. Perhaps he can earn the RB2 role behind the Big Dog, but the most likely outcome is that he is just a role player in Year 1.
Dynasty: Randall is huge (6’2”, 230+ lbs.), making him a unique evaluation for the NFL. We all love a good narrative and a chance to connect the dots for fantasy. We have seen some recent success from former WRs who converted to RB in the NFL, including Antonio Gibson, Tony Pollard, and Tyrone Tracy Jr. In rookie drafts, he was one of our favorite Round 3 lottery tickets.

Name: Demond Claiborne
Outlook: Claiborne slipped to the sixth round, albeit in a solid landing spot for the future, as both Aaron Jones Sr. and Jordan Mason are in the final year of their deals. His experience as a kick returner shows on the field, but dear Lord, Claiborne needs to take care of the football. He frequently was stuffed at the line of scrimmage, fumbled the football, or had concentration issues in the passing game.
Dynasty: If you are looking for lightning speed to get outside and make plays in space, Claiborne is a fun player with some boom-bust tendencies. Consider him a taxi squad all-star for 2026.

Name: Tahj Brooks

The Day 3 pick touched the ball 17 total times as a rookie, and old man Samaje Perine is back again in 2026 as the trusted third-down back the Bengals mix in.

Name: Audric Estimé
Outlook: After spending his rookie year in Denver, the one known as “Estime Lauder” found his way on the Saints roster and willed his way to two top-15 finishes in the final two weeks of the season. Before you get too excited, realize the entire Saints backfield depth chart was on injured reserve. Free agent signing Travis Etienne Jr. will be the bell-cow while Estimé occasionally mixes in for a handful of carries.
